title: [Delinearization] Extract outer array dimensions using global object size
author: sjoerdmeijer
description:
Use the size of a statically declared array to calculate the outer array
dimension. Obtaining this size and feeding that extra information into the
subscript recognition part helps to get more accurate results.

tests.test_body:
@test_array_10x20 = external global [10 x [20 x i32]]

define void @test_2d_array(i64 %i, i64 %j, i64 %N, i64 %M) {
entry:
  br label %for.i

for.i:                                            ; preds = %for.j.end, %entry
  %i.cur = phi i64 [ 0, %entry ], [ %i.next, %for.j.end ]
  %gepi = getelementptr inbounds nuw [20 x i32], ptr @test_array_10x20, i64 %i.cur
  %i.cmp = icmp slt i64 %i.cur, %N
  br i1 %i.cmp, label %for.j, label %exit

for.j:                                            ; preds = %for.j, %for.i
  %j.cur = phi i64 [ 0, %for.i ], [ %j.next, %for.j ]
  %gepij = getelementptr inbounds nuw i32, ptr %gepi, i64 %j.cur
  %val = load i32, ptr %gepij, align 4
  %j.next = add i64 %j.cur, 1
  %j.cmp = icmp slt i64 %j.next, %M
  br i1 %j.cmp, label %for.j, label %for.j.end

for.j.end:                                        ; preds = %for.j
  %i.next = add i64 %i.cur, 1
  br label %for.i

exit:                                             ; preds = %for.i
  ret void
}

define void @large_size_fixed(ptr %A) {
entry:
  br label %for.i.header

for.i.header:                                     ; preds = %for.i.latch, %entry
  %i = phi i64 [ 0, %entry ], [ %i.inc, %for.i.latch ]
  %i.mul = mul i64 %i, 256
  br label %for.j

for.j:                                            ; preds = %for.j, %for.i.header
  %j = phi i64 [ 0, %for.i.header ], [ %j.inc, %for.j ]
  %offset = add nsw i64 %i.mul, %j
  %gep = getelementptr i8, ptr %A, i64 %offset
  store i8 0, ptr %gep, align 1
  %j.inc = add i64 %j, 1
  %ec.j = icmp eq i64 %j.inc, 256
  br i1 %ec.j, label %for.i.latch, label %for.j

for.i.latch:                                      ; preds = %for.j
  %i.inc = add i64 %i, 1
  %ec.i = icmp eq i64 %i.inc, 1152921504606846976
  br i1 %ec.i, label %exit, label %for.i.header

exit:                                             ; preds = %for.i.latch
  ret void
}

define void @elementsize_cause_ovfl(ptr %A) {
entry:
  br label %for.i.header

for.i.header:                                     ; preds = %for.i.latch, %entry
  %i = phi i64 [ 0, %entry ], [ %i.inc, %for.i.latch ]
  %i.mul = mul i64 %i, 256
  br label %for.j

for.j:                                            ; preds = %for.j, %for.i.header
  %j = phi i64 [ 0, %for.i.header ], [ %j.inc, %for.j ]
  %offset = add i64 %i.mul, %j
  %gep = getelementptr i64, ptr %A, i64 %offset
  store i64 0, ptr %gep, align 4
  %j.inc = add i64 %j, 1
  %ec.j = icmp eq i64 %j.inc, 256
  br i1 %ec.j, label %for.i.latch, label %for.j

for.i.latch:                                      ; preds = %for.j
  %i.inc = add i64 %i, 1
  %ec.i = icmp eq i64 %i.inc, 18014398509481984
  br i1 %ec.i, label %exit, label %for.i.header

exit:                                             ; preds = %for.i.latch
  ret void
}

index 42d0eca0d..a50158aaf 100644
--- a/llvm/lib/Analysis/Delinearization.cpp
+++ b/llvm/lib/Analysis/Delinearization.cpp
@@ -711,9 +711,8 @@ bool llvm::delinearizeFixedSizeArray(ScalarEvolution &SE, const SCEV *Expr,
 
   // Maybe we can extract the outer dimension from the global variable
   // declaration.
-  bool 
-  if (BasePointer)
-    extractOuterDimFromGlobal(SE, BasePointer->getValue(), Sizes, ElementSize);
+  bool if (BasePointer) extractOuterDimFromGlobal(SE, BasePointer->getValue(),
+                                                  Sizes, ElementSize);
 
   // Second step: compute the access functions for each subscript.
   computeAccessFunctions(SE, Offset, Subscripts, Sizes);

comments.author: sjoerdmeijer
comments.body:
> Next to the functional change, this also changes the debug output in a way that makes it hard to see what is actually affected (looks like the access function includes the base pointer now and delinearization results are no longer validated? Why these changes? How are they related to the rest of the PR?)

Yeah, the changes in debug messages is maybe slightly unfortunately, I will see if I can reduce it.

The most important change is new test case `llvm/test/Analysis/Delinearization/global_array_bounds.ll`. I've created merge request #175173 to precommit that test case, so that changes are easier to see. I.e., it should show that delinearization currently fails:

```
; CHECK-NEXT:  AccessFunction: {{\{\{}}0,+,80}<nuw><nsw><%for.i>,+,4}<%for.j>
; CHECK-NEXT:  failed to delinearize
```

but with this change now succeeds:

```
; CHECK-NEXT:  AccessFunction: {{\{\{}}@test_array_10x20,+,80}<nw><%for.i>,+,4}<%for.j>
; CHECK-NEXT:  Base offset: @test_array_10x20
; CHECK-NEXT:  ArrayDecl[10][20] with elements of 4 bytes.
; CHECK-NEXT:  ArrayRef[{0,+,1}<nuw><nsw><%for.i>][{0,+,1}<nuw><nsw><%for.j>]
```

comments.author: Meinersbur
comments.body:
```ll
@test_array_10x20 = global [10 x [20 x i32]] zeroinitializer
```
There are 2 interpretations for this

1. This is an array of 10 arrays of 20 i32\'s

2. This is an sequence of 800 bytes (10\*20\*4)

The second interpretation would follow the trend of LLVM-IR going lower level, which could mean that eventually it might be replaced with an "allocate 800 bytes" instruction, in which case I fear we would eventually end up at the same problem we have with using the dimensional information from `getelementptr`. So what is the future direction?

However, information of delineratization could be interpreted as only a hint, not semantic, as e.g. Polly does. Runtime condition checks `0 <= j < 20` that for an access `test_array_10x20[i][j]`. If an execution would encounter a `j` outside this range, the non-optimized code is executed (i.e. loop versioning). For the optimized version it can assume that `i != i' && j != j'` implies `&test_array_10x20[i][j]` and `&test_array_10x20[i'][j']` do not alias. A hint could also be encoded a different way, which is what @sebpop is working on.

comments.author: sjoerdmeijer
comments.body:
> ```llvm
> @test_array_10x20 = global [10 x [20 x i32]] zeroinitializer
> ```
> 
> There are 2 interpretations for this
> 
> 1. This is an array of 10 arrays of 20 i32's
> 2. This is an sequence of 800 bytes (10*20*4)
> 
> The second interpretation would follow the trend of LLVM-IR going lower level, which could mean that eventually it might be replaced with an "allocate 800 bytes" instruction, in which case I fear we would eventually end up at the same problem we have with using the dimensional information from `getelementptr`. So what is the future direction?
> 
> However, information of delineratization could be interpreted as only a hint, not semantic, as e.g. Polly does. Runtime condition checks `0 <= j < 20` that for an access `test_array_10x20[i][j]`. If an execution would encounter a `j` outside this range, the non-optimized code is executed (i.e. loop versioning). For the optimized version it can assume that `i != i' && j != j'` implies `&test_array_10x20[i][j]` and `&test_array_10x20[i'][j']` do not alias. A hint could also be encoded a different way, which is what @sebpop is working on.

Agreed with everything you wrote here.

So, confirming that I fully accept that the direction of travel is the "this is just a bag of bytes" interpretation (i.e. interpretation 2). However, @nikic mentioned somewhere that this may take a while, and in the meantime, folks were okay not to block this. I will take over the work to emit a hint from the front-end, and thus will commit to the long-term solution. We would need to replace only some logic introduced here which will be straightforward once we have this mechanism.
This patch allows us to make some progress with getting our motivating examples and show an end-to-end approach to do this while I am going to look into those other things.

comments.author: kasuga-fj
comments.body:
> However, @nikic mentioned somewhere that this may take a while, and in the meantime, folks were okay not to block this.

Maybe [this one](https://discourse.llvm.org/t/rfc-de-type-ification-of-llvm-ir-why/88257/51)? If I understand correctly, allocas/globals are already just bags of bytes and type information carries almost no semantics. For delinearization, type information is only a hint, so we still need to validate the delinearization result (e.g., range checks) even if it's recovered from type information. If the loop bounds aren't compile-time constants, I think the validation will fail in most cases.  That’s why I don’t think driving delinearization based on type information is particularly useful.

comments.author: sjoerdmeijer
comments.body:
> > However, @nikic mentioned somewhere that this may take a while, and in the meantime, folks were okay not to block this.
> 
> Maybe [this one](https://discourse.llvm.org/t/rfc-de-type-ification-of-llvm-ir-why/88257/51)? If I understand correctly, allocas/globals are already just bags of bytes and type information carries almost no semantics. For delinearization, type information is only a hint, so we still need to validate the delinearization result (e.g., range checks) even if it's recovered from type information. If the loop bounds aren't compile-time constants, I think the validation will fail in most cases. That’s why I don’t think driving delinearization based on type information is particularly useful.

It is useful as test case `llvm/test/Analysis/Delinearization/global_array_bounds.ll` is clearly demonstrating.
Repeating what I mentioned before: the IR simplification is direction of travel, but this hasn't happened yet for globals and it will take a while. I believe we all agree on that. 
@kasuga-fj: let me ask a direct question: are you blocking this work? I am asking because I feel we have discussed all angles here already.

comments.author: kasuga-fj
comments.body:
No, it's not that I want to block this, but I don't think that `llvm/test/Analysis/Delinearization/global_array_bounds.ll` can be recovered even if using type information. What's ongoing, as far as I understand, is the effort to completely eliminate representations like `[10 x [20 x i8]]`. Even if we have a declaration like `@arr = global [10 x [20 x i32]]`, there’s no longer any guarantee that `@arr` will actually be accessed in that way (e.g., the following LLVM should be valid). But maybe I'm missing something.

```llvm
@arr = global [10 x [20 x i8]] zeroinitializer

; for (int i = 0; i < 4; i++)
;   for (int j = 0; j < 42; j++)
;     arr[i*42 + j] = 0;
;
define void @f() {
entry:
  br label %loop.i.header

loop.i.header:
  %i = phi i64 [ 0, %entry ], [ %i.inc, %loop.i.latch ]
  br label %loop.j

loop.j:
  %j = phi i64 [ 0, %loop.i.header ], [ %j.inc, %loop.j ]
  %gep = getelementptr [42 x i8], ptr @arr, i64 %i, i64 %j
  store i8 0, ptr %gep
  %j.inc = add i64 %j, 1
  %ec.j = icmp eq i64 %j.inc, 42
  br i1 %ec.j, label %loop.i.latch, label %loop.j

loop.i.latch:
  %i.inc = add i64 %i, 1
  %ec.i = icmp eq i64 %i.inc, 4
  br i1 %ec.i, label %exit, label %loop.i.header

exit:
  ret void
}
```

comments.author: nikic
comments.body:
What's important to me here is that we don't consider type based heuristics unless...

 * ...there is a real-world case, where...
 * ...it is infeasible to delinearize without type information (!= the current implementation cannot delinearize)...
 * ...and the delinearization based on the type information can actually be used (i.e. does not just run into a validation failure anyway).

I do not want to block this direction *categorically*, but I'm struggling to understand why we need this for the motivating test case. I'm probably missing something here, but it looks to me like it should be possible to get the same delinearization result without the type information, but even if we produced it, we'd fail to validate it due to the variable loop bounds anyway.

comments.author: sjoerdmeijer
comments.body:
> What's important to me here is that we don't consider type based heuristics unless...
> 
> * ...there is a real-world case, where...
> * ...it is infeasible to delinearize without type information (!= the current implementation cannot delinearize)...
> * ...and the delinearization based on the type information can actually be used (i.e. does not just run into a validation failure anyway).
> 
> I do not want to block this direction _categorically_, but I'm struggling to understand why we need this for the motivating test case. I'm probably missing something here, but it looks to me like it should be possible to get the same delinearization result without the type information, but even if we produced it, we'd fail to validate it due to the variable loop bounds anyway.

Ok, thanks, understood & agreed. 

I will first look into your suggestion made in #175173. Let's tackle that small test case first, then I will look if that also helps our motivating examples. I will keep this open for now, and will close this when this turns out not to be necessary.

comments.author: sjoerdmeijer
comments.body:
Thanks again for the suggestion, @nikic , @kasuga-fj.
This now looks at the global object size to determine the outer dimension, and is thus not relying on any type information anymore.

comments.author: kasuga-fj
comments.body: Could you fix the compile errors first?
